source_metadata.title_raw: Julia M. Lew - A-19-178 - December 12, 2019 - Visalia

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

December 12, 2019

Julia M. Lew
4010 South Demarce St.
Visalia, CA 93277

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-19-178

Dear Ms. Lew:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Ponderosa Community Services
District Board Member Robert Cope regarding Government Code Section 1090, et seq.¹ Please
note that we are only providing advice under Section 1090, not under other general conflict of
interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest, including Public Contract Code.

Also, note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1
FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for
additional advice.

We are required to forward your request regarding Section 1090 and all pertinent facts
relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the Tulare County District Attorney's
Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.1(c)(3).) We did not receive a written response from
either entity. (Section 1097.1(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, for purposes of
Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any
individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.1(c)(5).)

QUESTIONS

1. May Mr. Cope work for High Sierra on contracts between High Sierra and private
property owners located within the District and, if so, is he required to disqualify himself from
District decisions involving private property owners in the District who contract with High Sierra
for services including owners for whom Mr. Cope performed work on High Sierra's behalf?

2. May Mr. Cope work for High Sierra pursuant to the existing snow removal contract
between the District and High Sierra or a long-standing agreement between the District and Tulare
County to annually reimburse the County for a portion of the costs of services provided by High
Sierra?

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-19-178
Page No. 2

3. May the District renew its contract with High Sierra during Mr. Cope's tenure as a board
member or amend or execute any agreement between the District and Tulare County for
reimbursement for the snow removal services?

CONCLUSIONS

1. Section 1090 prohibits an official from making or participating in the making of a
contract by the official's agency in which the official has a financial interest. It does not prohibit
Mr. Cope from working for High Sierra on contracts with private persons.²

2. As stated above, Section 1090 prohibits an official from making or participating in the
making of a contract by the official's agency in which the official has a financial interest. Section
1090 does not prohibit Mr. Cope from performing services under a contract between the District
and High Sierra or a contract between the District and the County to reimburse for service performed
by High Sierra, so long as the contract was executed prior to Mr. Cope's election to office.

3. While Section 1090 potentially prohibits the execution of a new or renewed contract
between the District and High Sierra, or between the District and the County to reimburse the
County for services performed by High Sierra, in this case Section 1090 does not prohibit the
District from executing a new contract or renewing an existing contract because the rule of
necessity applies. However, Mr. Cope must recuse himself from any decisions involving the
contract.

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

The Ponderosa Community Services District (located in the Sequoia National Monument)
provides water to the improved District properties, and snow removal services for the main roads
within the District that lead to the District properties. The District is small, and has 327 total lots
within the District. The District has a five-member board elected at large. All of the lots are
required to pay the District for the snow removal service of the public right of way located within
the District, and 146 lots receive water service.

The District hires a third-party snow removal contractor for the snow removal service it
provides. Mr. Cope works for High Sierra,³ which has the multi-year snow removal contract with
the District. However, High Sierra also contracts with private property owners within the District to
provide snow removal services for their driveways, and does additional general contracting and
snow removal work within the District and the surrounding area. Additionally, High Sierra has a
contract with Tulare County for snow removal services for the portions of the Western Divide
Highway and Aspen Road that lead to the District roads, and the District shares in that cost and
provides annual reimbursement to the County for a portion of those services. Tulare County is

2 The Act prohibits Mr. Cope form taking part in governmental decisions with a reasonably foreseeable
material effect on an interest in a business entity or a source of income. You have indicated that Mr. Cope does not have
an ownership interest in High Sierra, according to him he does not have a potentially disqualifying interest in High Sierra's
clients and is not disqualified from taking part in a governmental decision with a financial effect on the clients assuming
Mr. Cope has no other interest in the decision.

³ In a subsequent telephone call, you noted that Mr. Cope does not have any ownership interest in High Sierra.

File No. A-19-178
Page No. 3

initially responsible for up to $6,000 of the costs, with the County and the District splitting the
additional costs on a 50/50 basis. This typically results in an annual payment by the District to
Tulare County in the range of $20,000 to $25,000.

Due to the District's remote location, it is extremely difficult to find other snow removal
contractors willing to perform the District's work, and it is difficult for the contractor to hire
qualified employees. You note that the District advertised this contract several times over the years.
The original contractor retained for snow removal has gone out of business. The District has since
contracted with High Sierra, with the exception of one other time, when it accepted a bid from
another contractor. This was the only other contractor besides High Sierra who has ever submitted a
bid. This contractor was located 30 minutes away from Ponderosa, and the contract was a disaster
for the community, as this contractor was barely able to finish out one season. Since then the
District has never received so much as an inquiry from a different contractor, and you are not aware
of any other contractors in the area that offer snow removal services. The only other option is the
County. If the District contracted with them, it would likely contract with High Sierra to remove
snow, as High Sierra already contracts with the County for snow removal, and the District would
pay much more than it is now.

ANALYSIS

Section 1090

Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities,
from making contracts in which they are financially interested. Section 1090 is concerned with
financial interests, other than remote or minimal interests, that prevent public officials from
exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their
agencies. (Stigall v. Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) Section 1090 is intended not only to strike at
actual impropriety, but also to strike at the appearance of impropriety. (City of Imperial Beach v.
Bailey (1980) 103 Cal.App.3d 191, 197.)

Under Section 1090, the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official has a
financial interest. (People v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) A contract that violates
Section 1090 is void. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 33 Cal.3d 633, 646.) The prohibition applies
regardless of whether the terms of the contract are fair and equitable to all parties. (Id. at pp. 646-
649.)

Contracts Between High Sierra and Private Property Owners

You initially ask whether Mr. Cope can work for High Sierra on snow removal or other
contracts between High Sierra and private property owners located within the District. Because
these contracts do not involve the District, Mr. Cope may perform work on them and High Sierra
may continue to enter such contracts.

Contracts Between High Sierra and the County/District

Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities,
from making or participating in making contracts in which they are financially interested.

File No. A-19-178
Page No. 4

Therefore, Section 1090 does not prohibit Mr. Cope from working for High Sierra under an existing contract between the District and High Sierra or and existing contract between the District and County to reimburse the County for services provided by High Sierra.

In regard to a decision involving a new contract or renewal of an existing contract with High Sierra, or the County to reimburse the County for services performed by High Sierra, Mr. Cope has a financial interest because his employer, High Sierra, will financially benefit from the contracts. Accordingly, Mr. Cope is prohibited from making or participating in the contracts.

Additionally, when board members have the power to execute contracts, participation is constructive. Thus, where an official is a member of a board or commission that has the power to execute the contract, he or she is presumed to be involved in the making of his or her agency's contracts irrespective of whether he or she actually participates in the making of the contract. (Thomson v. Call, supra, at pp. 645 & 649.). Absent an applicable exception, such as the rule of necessity analyzed below, the District is prohibited from executing a new contract or renewing an existing contracting with High Sierra or the County to reimburse the County for services performed by High Sierra.

Rule of Necessity

Despite the Section 1090 prohibitions above, you have asked whether the "rule of necessity" may apply to allow the District to enter into contracts with High Sierra and the County. In limited cases, the "rule of necessity" has been applied to allow the making of a contract that Section 1090 would otherwise prohibit. (Eldridge v. Sierra View Hospital Dist. (1990) 224 Cal. App. 3d 311, 322.) The rule has been applied where public policy concerns authorize the contract and "ensures that essential government functions are performed even where a conflict of interest exists." (Id.; see also 88 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 106, 110 (2005).)

The rule of necessity has been applied in at least two specific types of situations: 1) in procurement situations for essential supplies or services when no source other than the one that triggers the conflict is available; and 2) in non-procurement situations to carry out essential duties of the office when the official or board is the only one authorized to act.

It is important to note that the rule of necessity has only been applied in very limited situations. For example, a city could obtain emergency nighttime services from a service station owned by a member of the city council, where the town was isolated, and the council member's station was the only one in the area that was open. (4 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 264 (1944).) Also, a healthcare district in a remote area could advertise its services on a local radio station, even though one of the district's directors was employed at the station. After exploring other outlets, it was clear that the radio station was the only source that would deliver the necessary information in an efficient, cost-effective, and timely manner. (88 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 106 (2005).)

What these situations have in common is the exigency of the circumstances such that delaying action to contract with a non-conflicted source would be to the detriment of the affected people. (See Ramos Advice Letter, No. A-14-105). As stated above, the "rule of necessity" applies when there is no alternative source for essential services and when necessary to carry out essential duties.

File No. A-19-178
Page No. 5

Here, you note that the District is located within the boundaries of the Sequoia National Monument, in a remote and sparsely populated area. The District bears the responsibility of providing snow removal for the roads within its boundaries. However, no one other than High Sierra has expressed an interest in bidding on the contract for snow removal in recent years, and you also state that you are not aware of any other contractors in the area that offer snow removal services.

Based on these facts, and consistent with applicable law, we therefore conclude that the rule of necessity applies, and the District may enter into a renewal contract for snow removal with the County and with High Sierra. However, Mr. Cope must not make or participate in making any decisions related to the contracts.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

[signature]

By: Zachary W. Norton
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

ZWN:aja
